Here am I, send me.<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> may be more familiar to some of you from a hymn than it actually is from Scripture.<|7.54|> <|8.98|> It was made so very popular in the 1980s through a Catholic liturgical song,<|15.98|> <|16.90|> The Lord of Sea and Sky, and it's one that is still used at many ordinations.<|23.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.64|> These call stories are well known to anyone who has sought holy orders.<|9.80|> <|10.54|> When going through discernment, you are asked again and again about your call.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.86|> And I am certain that our three postulants, Amanda and Chad and Christina, are having<|8.18|> <|8.18|> uncomfortable flashbacks just hearing these words.<|11.40|> <|12.36|> Christina is nodding.<|13.38|> <|13.48|> She's standing right over here.<|14.62|> <|16.64|> It's one of the most awkward, well, I speak for myself, for me, it was one of the most<|23.62|> <|23.62|> awkward parts of discernment.<|25.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It feels so strange to tell people that you feel called by God.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> It's not a way we are used to speaking, at least not as Episcopalians.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> It feels like you are some strange cocktail of ego and insanity.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.40|> Poor Isaiah.<|3.84|> <|5.10|> Imagine how he must feel having to share his call story.<|8.64|> <|9.20|> Winged, singing serpents.<|11.30|> <|14.54|> I am wary of these stories becoming the precious territory of those who are<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> are called to the ordained life. These stories are for all of us. We are all called to something.<|9.96|> <|11.00|> We all have a ministry as the baptized. Why are we so sheepish about believing, much less saying<|22.18|> <|22.18|> that we are called to something?<|25.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I challenge you all to practice it.<|2.34|> <|3.84|> Long before I was called to the priesthood,<|6.86|> <|7.36|> I was called to be a high school English teacher.<|10.46|> <|11.60|> Then I was called to motherhood.<|13.52|> <|14.76|> And within the church, I was called to serve as a vestry member<|19.30|> <|19.30|> and a senior warden.<|20.92|> <|21.32|> I was called to lead small groups and book studies,<|24.22|> <|24.50|> to serve the chalice and to serve on the altar.<|27.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I was called to be a lector and an acolyte.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> I was called to be a Eucharistic visitor.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> I had 25 years of being called as a lay leader before I was called to the priesthood.<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I love that these call stories have appeared on the days of our vestry election last week<|8.64|> <|8.64|> and our vestry installation this week.<|11.78|> <|12.34|> I'm grateful to all of those who have said yes.<|17.40|> <|18.46|> Yes to serving this parish for thanksgiving.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> three years committing to prayer, service, bringing your best self, your mind, your giftedness,<|10.60|> <|10.60|> and your time to the service of God's people at St. John's Cathedral.
